metadata.title: The Creative Engineer's Market — How AI Is Repricing Professional Value
metadata.purpose: Show students in Nina Harris' Branding and AI course how Claude synthesizes the labor-market evidence for AI-era repricing of professional value, ending with a field-by-field table of pooled vs. currently-separating signals.
metadata.audience: Students and practitioners in a university branding course with an AI component
sources: Branding and AI, Chapter 1 — The Creative Engineer (Nina Harris)

skin_warnings: B00: palette=claude but the cold open is 'ClaudeComposerAsk916' — COLD OPEN LAW wants ClaudeComposerAsk
END: palette=claude but the outro is 'un-annotated' — OUTRO LAW wants ClaudeTitleOutro

metadata.reformat: center-cut, focus-aware; pantry/<bid>-916.* is the human replacement slot; -916 overrides honored

beats.narration_text: Olá — this is Liam, in for Bear. For Nina Harris' course, Branding and AI, here is the question we are running today: the market is repricing. AI engineering base salaries average two hundred six thousand dollars, with specialists pulling thirty to fifty percent above generalists at the same seniority. The question is what the market is paying for now that it was not paying for before — and which of your credentials are still part of the answer.

props.command: Research the labor-market evidence for the Creative Engineer repricing hypothesis: Peng et al. 2023 GitHub Copilot results, AI salary premiums (specialist vs. generalist), evidence from design/marketing/writing, Spence signaling mechanism. End with field-by-field table: Old signal / Now pooled / Currently separating.
output: Peng et al.: 56% task-completion reduction for control group vs. Copilot
Design, marketing, writing: same pooling pattern

beats.narration_text: Here is what breaks without this map. You invest professional development time in credentials that felt like the right answer two years ago. The market has already repriced them. You find out when the offer comes in below your expectations, or when the interview question turns to something you did not prepare for. The Spence mechanism says a signal works only as long as its cost structure holds. AI tooling is the largest single-year cost-structure collapse in the history of professional credentials.

props.sparkLine: AI tooling is the largest single-year credential cost-structure collapse.

beats.narration_text: The theory from Chapter One. Spence's signaling mechanism applied to the AI era: a signal separates candidates only when its cost structure prevents cheap imitation. Peng et al. 2023 GitHub Copilot RCT: control group completed fifty-six percent fewer tasks than the Copilot group. This is the cost-structure data — AI tooling halved the effort required to produce the same code output. The market response: AI engineering base salaries average two hundred and six thousand dollars with specialists pulling thirty to fifty percent above generalists. The market is paying for the judgment that decides what to build, not the tooling that builds it.

Honesty about what the synthesis does not do. Labor market pricing is dynamic — currently-separating signals will be the next signals to pool as AI tooling advances. The table is a current-state map, not permanent terrain. And the synthesis is grounded in the evidence base from Chapter One — the specific numbers, the Peng et al. result, the salary premium — not generic claims about AI and jobs.

artifactLines: Labor market pricing is dynamic — currently-separating signals will pool next.
The table is a current-state map, not permanent terrain.
The synthesis is grounded in specific evidence — not generic AI-and-jobs claims.
The repricing is still underway — the map will need updating within two years.

beats.narration_text: Let us recap with Claude. The Creative Engineer's Market Repricing. Four lines.

artifactLines: Peng et al. 2023: AI tooling compressed code-production cost by roughly half.
The market response: $206K base, 30–50% specialist premium for judgment roles.
Five fields, same pattern: production pooled, judgment separates.
The currently-separating signals will pool next — the map needs updating.

beats.narration_text: Your turn. Take this prompt into your Claude session: I am a practitioner in the field of blank. Here is my current credential set and skill profile: describe what you currently have. Apply the field-by-field repricing table to my specific context: which of my current credentials are now in the pooled column? Which skills I have are in the currently-separating column? What is the one investment I should make in the next twelve months to move toward the currently-separating signals in my field — based on the Spence mechanism and the labor market evidence? Name what you are good at first. Then name what has already pooled. That gap is the answer.

props.command: Apply the Creative Engineer repricing map to my field: [FIELD]. My current credentials: [CREDENTIAL SET]. Which are now pooled? Which of my skills are in the currently-separating column? What is the one investment I should make in the next 12 months based on the Spence mechanism?

beats.narration_text: That's the short version. The full video also covers The centerpiece: field-by-field repricing table.… — watch The Creative Engineer's Market — How AI Is Repricing Professional Value for the whole story. The link is right below.
